posts.name: Eric S n y d e r
posts.cooked:
2015 is getting a lot of press all over Europe, but my biggest interest lies in the Rhone. That said, it’s a relatively young love, and I’m curious to hear what you veterans are buying, both for for early drinking and cellaring.

So far, I’ve purchased:
Faury St Joseph VV
Graillot Croze-Hermitage
Vincent Paris Cornas Le Geynale

I haven’t bought any Southern Rhones yet - even less experience there. So far, I like but don’t love them (in general), but I’m curious what others are into.

Envoyer just sent out an offer for the Voge Les Chailles @ $50, which sounds like a higher price than years past, but this is 2015 after all.
posts.display_username: Eric S n y d e r

posts.name: Arv R@()
posts.cooked: What happened to the various Chapoutier No. Rhone offerings? Many of my usual vendors when from the 2014 offerings to the 2016 pre arrivals without showing the 2015’s.

posts.name: John Morris
posts.cooked: The few lower level wines from the Northern Rhone that I’ve had have been very good: Vincent Paris’s St. Joseph and Barou’s St. Joseph and VdP Ardeche syrah. Definitely ripe, with boatloads of fruit, but not over the top for my somewhat AFWE palate.

posts.name: Alan Rath
posts.cooked:
Eric S n y d e r:

So far, I’ve purchased:
Faury St Joseph VV
Graillot Croze-Hermitage
Vincent Paris Cornas Le Geynale

I haven’t bought any Southern Rhones yet - even less experience there. So far, I like but don’t love them (in general), but I’m curious what others are into.

I’ve tasted the Graillot, and the “regular” Faury, and they were both very good (I slightly preferred the Faury). I would expect the Faury VV to be outstanding. Geynale should also be terrific, though I haven’t yet had it.

I swore of almost all southern Rhones some years ago. I would only touch them in a much cooler year, which 15 is not.

posts.name: Brian S t o t t e r
posts.cooked:
Love Southern Rhone GSMs. Will likely get Vieux Telegraphe CdP La Crau, Beaucastel CdP, and Le Vieux Donjon CdP.

For someone who hasn’t had much Northern Rhone experience, what producers/regions would be a good starting point?
posts.display_username: Brian S t o t t e r

posts.name: Eric S n y d e r
posts.cooked:
Brian S t o t t e r:

Love Southern Rhone GSMs. Will likely get Vieux Telegraphe CdP La Crau, Beaucastel CdP, and Le Vieux Donjon CdP.

For someone who hasn’t had much Northern Rhone experience, what producers/regions would be a good starting point?

My first couple Northern Rhones were Cote Rotie, which is a bit like learning to swim in the deep end. Wouldn’t recommend that. Try a St Joseph from well known producer (like Domaine Chave), preferably with a few years on it. 2011’s are drinking great right now.

Brian S t o t t e r:

For someone who hasn’t had much Northern Rhone experience, what producers/regions would be a good starting point?

posts.name: Robert Alfert
posts.cooked: Havent grabbed any yet, Arv. Usually from Crush.

posts.name: John Morris
posts.cooked:
Eric S n y d e r:
Brian S t o t t e r:

Love Southern Rhone GSMs. Will likely get Vieux Telegraphe CdP La Crau, Beaucastel CdP, and Le Vieux Donjon CdP.

For someone who hasn’t had much Northern Rhone experience, what producers/regions would be a good starting point?

My first couple Northern Rhones were Cote Rotie, which is a bit like learning to swim in the deep end. Wouldn’t recommend that. Try a St Joseph from well known producer (like Domaine Chave), preferably with a few years on it. 2011’s are drinking great right now.

Yes, probably better to start with some of the more approachable wines. 2015 is a good vintage to stick your toes in the Northern Rhone waters, since it’s not short on fruit. Among the “lesser” wines that shouldn’t be too hard to find or too expensive, I’d suggest:

Clape - Vin des Amis
Jaboulet - Dom. Thalabert - Crozes Hermitage
Vincent Paris - St. Joseph
Alain Graillot - Crozes Hermitage

(Some of the wines on people’s lists here, like Gonon and Allemand, are very hard to get your hands on.)

posts.name: M. Dildine
posts.cooked:
Rhone “Rangers.”

I’me a locavore (local yokel)!

posts.name: Alan Rath
posts.cooked:
Brian S t o t t e r:

For someone who hasn’t had much Northern Rhone experience, what producers/regions would be a good starting point?

The “regular” Faury St Joseph (not VV) is really good, pretty open for drinking, relatively affordable and easy to find. Try that one and see how you like it before diving in too deep. If you want to turn it up a notch, another wine to try is Clape’s Renaissance.

And don’t limit yourself to 2015. 2013 was an excellent year (slightly under the radar), and 2014 got snubbed by the critics. It was indeed a more difficult year, but good producers made some very fine wines.

posts.name: Robert Alfert
posts.cooked:
What’s the deal with Domaine Courbis? Style? Some of the notes sounds pretty modern, glossy, but I’ve never had it.

If you like them a little glossy and riper, Voge and Paris 30/60 are solid buys. Not my style, but total crowd pleasers.

posts.name: Daniel Kim
posts.cooked:
Robert Alfert:

What’s the deal with Domaine Courbis? Style? Some of the notes sounds pretty modern, glossy, but I’ve never had it.

If you like them a little glossy and riper, Voge and Paris 30/60 are solid buys. Not my style, but total crowd pleasers.

I’d say it’s similar to Voge and Paris.

TBH, besides Allemand, Juge and Clape, Cornas producers seem to be all producing the same style. It’s hard to distinguish them young, and I drink a lot of N. Rhone.

2015 I’m planning on buying or have bought:

Cote Rotie: Jamet, Level, Barge, Texier, Benetiere, Clusel-Roch, and Gallet
Hermitage: Faurie, Graillot, Greal and maybe Chave
Cornas: Juge, Allemand
St. Joseph: Gonon, Faury, Domaine de Misquettes, and Perret

posts.name: Alan Rath
posts.cooked: A new one to me in 2015: Graillot St Joseph, is quite good.

posts.name: Nicos Neocleous
posts.cooked: Cote-Rotie from Ogier.

posts.name: Robert Alfert
posts.cooked:
Alan Rath:

If you want to turn it up a notch, another wine to try is Clape’s Renaissance.

And don’t limit yourself to 2015. 2013 was an excellent year (slightly under the radar), and 2014 got snubbed by the critics. It was indeed a more difficult year, but good producers made some very fine wines.

While I have selectively purchased Clape’s Renaissance, like in a killer year like 2010, I’ve generally believed it is over-priced, and I’d rather just pay the additional premium for the main Cornas bottling.

I concur on looking at vintages that remain available, 2013 being one of them. An strong vintage. I also really like 2011, and some stuff is still out there.

posts.name: Alan Rath
posts.cooked:
Robert Alfert:

While I have selectively purchased Clape’s Renaissance, like in a killer year like 2010, I’ve generally believed it is over-priced, and I’d rather just pay the additional premium for the main Cornas bottling.

I fully understand. But for those of us non-lawyers who can’t just flip some art when they want to buy more wine, $60 vs $100 makes a difference And the Renaissance is a great bottle of Syrah, I think extremely competitive in its price range

posts.name: Bryan Carr
posts.cooked:
Was just there and picked some up, though we both prefer lighter wines so we grabbed many '14s as well.

Our acquisitions thus far:

Franck Balthazar Chaillot
Rostaing Ampodium
Rostaing La Landonne
Rostaing Cote Blonde
Dom. du Tunnel Cornas
Alain Voge Cornas VV
Clos des Papes

Those Rostaing wines were just unbelievably beautiful, stars of the trip by a long shot.

Was surprised at how much wasn’t ready for sale when we were there, Clusel Roch, Verset, Delas, Telegraphe and more were still sitting on their '15s, or hadn’t even bottled them yet. The bottling truck was cranking away outside of Pegau when we were there.

I know people are mixed on '14 but we found many of the better producers made wines with considerable finesse and grace, the Delas Bessards was impeccable and the Telegraphe La Crau was downright ethereal for a CdP.

posts.name: James Billy
posts.cooked:
Arv R:
Brian S t o t t e r:

For someone who hasn’t had much Northern Rhone experience, what producers/regions would be a good starting point?

I think Guigal’s regular blends are solid and widely available; the base Crozes and St Joseph are reliable value as well.

I’m not a AFWE zealot, but I can really taste oak in a bad way with most Guigal reds. I’m not a fan.

Definitely like Faury St Joe.

Question is: which reasonably priced, middle of the road (in a good way) are you backing the truck up for?

posts.name: Matt Mauldin
posts.cooked: Great so far for ‘drink me nows’ in the $20-$30ish price range. The Graillot Crozes Hermitage 2015 was a sick value at the $30’s price range when I had it. Just had a Beaucastel Coudoulet de Beaucastel that was quite tasty.

posts.name: Kristoffer B r y d e r
posts.cooked:
Arv R:
Brian S t o t t e r:

For someone who hasn’t had much Northern Rhone experience, what producers/regions would be a good starting point?

I think Guigal’s regular blends are solid and widely available; the base Crozes and St Joseph are reliable value as well.

I don’t know much about availability in the states but Cave de Tain also have holdings in a lot of N. Rhône appellations, except for Cote-Rotie and Condrieu. The few 2015’s I’ve tasted from there have been very elegant and fresh, and not marred by heat in any way. The 2011’s from St. Joseph and Crozes-Hermitage are already very pleasurable!

posts.name: Arv R@()

Brian S t o t t e r:

For someone who hasn’t had much Northern Rhone experience, what producers/regions would be a good starting point?

I think Guigal’s regular blends are solid and widely available; the base Crozes and St Joseph are reliable value as well.

I don’t know much about availability in the states but Cave de Tain also have holdings in a lot of N. Rhône appellations, except for Cote-Rotie and Condrieu. The few 2015’s I’ve tasted from there have been very elegant and fresh, and not marred by heat in any way. The 2011’s from St. Joseph and Crozes-Hermitage are already very pleasurable!

Total Wine carries some of the Cave de Tain offerings, like their base syrah and marsanne offerings, as well as their Crozes but the none of the fancier bottlings seem to show up in our location, other than off vintages of the Gambert Loche Hermitage. Is there anyone in the US who carries their full line up? (sort of like how PJ’s in NYC carries most of the CVNE list)

posts.name: Andrew K0t0wski
posts.cooked:
Matt Mauldin:

Great so far for ‘drink me nows’ in the $20-$30ish price range. The Graillot Crozes Hermitage 2015 was a sick value at the $30’s price range when I had it. Just had a Beaucastel Coudoulet de Beaucastel that was quite tasty.

The Graillot Crozes continues to be a home run at that price point; I probably went through two cases last year. Weird thing about the mags (at least here) is that they are a couple of euros cheaper than buying two bottles separately.

Other nice Crozes at ~$25 point is the Domaine Les Alexandrins. It’s not as good as the Graillot, but it’s a beast of a Syrah.

On the higher end, am definitely a fan of Rostaing. I’ve had a couple of the La Landonnes with age on them and they were spectacular.

posts.name: John Morris
posts.cooked:
Matt Mauldin:

Just had a Beaucastel Coudoulet de Beaucastel that was quite tasty.

I agree, based on a bottle on Wednesday evening. It was quite drinkable now but had enough grip that I suspect it will age 5 to 8 years at least.

posts.name: Ryan Caughey
posts.cooked:
Brian G r a f s t r o m:
Alan Rath:
Robert Alfert:

Just took my own advice, backfilled on a few 2010 Ogier Cotie Rotie. B21 for $90.

This one is a little surprising. Good, but more modern in style - though I hear he has backed off on new oak.

It’s looking like N. Rhone has gone over the edge in pricing. Clape beyond what I’ll pay. Jamet well beyond. Gonon gone nuts. I expect 15 Allemand to hit or exceed $200, at least for Reynard. Still a few up-and-comers and under-the-radar producers, but sadly I have to be done with too many of the long time standard bearers. Sigh.

Yup. Hopefully this is a bubble akin to Bdx. circa 2009 and 2010. No. Rhone just feels very faddish to me right now; how many folks were talking N. Rhone on this board even 5 years ago?

N. Rhone is indeed trendy but I don’t see the bottom falling out. The difference between N. Rhone and Bordeaux is the small production numbers. Benetiere makes 350 cases/yr. Allemand’s total annual production is 650 cases. Levet’s is 1,000 cases. And some–like Juge, Jamet Brune, Benetiere Dolium–are way less than that. There’s not the same risk with Bordeaux that a couple down years suppress demand to the point that there is excess supply. And it doesn’t take a lot of people interested in these wines to keep the momentum. I’m not a speculator at all – never sold a bottle, in fact – but I think it’s more likely that we see further escalation of the most sought after wines, than it is that we see pricing declines.

posts.name: Robert Alfert
posts.cooked:
c fu:

Or, more attention is being put on it with more importers, more critics and people are realizing the wines are pretty damn tasty and diverse from every distinct region in N. Rhone. When people say the guys in Burgundy are just farmers, it’s even more so in N. Rhone.

I agree with this.

For me, I have sorta quietly been stocking up on Rhones over the past 10 years but definitely with an escalation these last few years (in large part due to a great run of quality vintages). Following my orders these last 2 months deep into 2015s, I would not be surprised that Northern Rhones are now my largest holding. I got the Rhone bug first time I tried Clape Cornas in the mid-90s. But, living in FL, it was super hard to find these wines. Only when FL finally started allowing private in-state shipments, did I start buying more and broadly from the specialty retailers up in the NE; it took time to find the right retailers and then get on “lists” for wines like Juge and now even Gonon. I do not think it is faddish at all, I think it is reality. This is the new, new. Northern Rhone is in the spotlight, lots of new and young people buying them in spades, small production, artisanal wineries, and some legends like Juge retiring. I do not see things changing in the near-term. Hold on to your wallets . . . .

posts.name: John Morris
posts.cooked: The good thing is that there is still a lot of under-realized potential in the better parts of St. Joseph and Crozes-Hermitage, unlike the big three zones – Cote Rotie, Hermitage and Cornas – which are very small and fully exploited. Also, you don’t have dominant producers with big market shares in the “lesser” applications, as you do in Hermitage and Cote Rotie.

Frankly, I would argue that there are a number of excellent California producers (and possibly Washington, but I don’t follow those) that are making wines approaching the quality of the top St Josephs. It’s not a big number, but they are out there.

posts.name: John Morris
posts.cooked:
How many of you who are loading up on '15 Northern Rhones have tasted them?

Until tonight, the only '15s I’d had were a couple of lovely St. Josephs (Barou and V. Paris). But my blind tasting group just tasted eight Northern Rhones and they were all VERY ripe. I mean REALLY ripe. Black cherry/blackberry jam.

I’ve been tasting Northern Rhones on release since the 1983 vintage, and these are far and away the ripest, most atypical wines I’ve ever had at this stage. (I didn’t taste the ‘03s early on.) I’m not sure these wines will age for long. I fear they are just too ripe, with too little acid and too soft tannins. The best are luscious and chuggable now. But 10 years from now? Risky, I’d say. It seems like the vintage served up critics’ wines – wines that will garner lots of points.

It’s too late in my day to post detailed notes now, but someone said early on in tonight’s tasting, “If you’d told me these were California syrahs, I’d have believed you.” The Barruol-Lynch (a joint venture with Kermit Lynch) Crozes was more like Aussie shiraz – blackberry and black cherry jam with an oak overlay. (OK, a restrained shiraz.) Et tu, Kermit?

I’ll post notes tomorrow, but before you back up the truck, I suggest trying a few of these to see if they’re really your style.

The wines we tasted were from traditionalists and you might expect St. Joseph and Crozes to show less ripeness than, say, Hermitage in a warm vintage. Not so with this line-up:

Dard & Ribo - Crozes
Barruol-Lynch - Crozes
Balthazar - Cornas sans souffre
Vincent Paris - Cornas - Granit 60
Faury - Cote Rotie
Faury - St Joseph VV
Herve Souhaut - St. Joseph - Les Cessieux
Gilles Barges - St. Joseph

posts.name: Arv R@()
posts.cooked:
I think that’s a very fair criticism/observation. I’ll freely admit that I have not tasted most of what I’m laying down. If I was in a environment/locale where I could rustle up syrah enthusiasts and pull something off like your panel, I’d love that, and would prefer shopping with more tasting experience of what I’m actually cluttering up space with.

But I won’t make the perfect the enemy of the good. Personally, I loved the fat – and atypical – 2003 N.Rhones so if the 2015 works out that way, I’ll be happy. Didn’t care for the southern Rhones that year, and found them increasingly unbalanced as time went on, and glad that I didn’t load up, having already gorged on the 98-01 CNDP years.

Look forward to seeing your notes.

posts.name: Daniel Paik
posts.cooked:
The '15 Rousset St. Joseph Picaudieres is drinking pretty nicely in my opinion. Ripe, yes, but in a good way. Although maybe a little soft and plush too. Much preferred to the '14.

That being said, I do freely admit to buying a few '15 wines without tasting.

posts.name: Pat_Martin
posts.cooked: Thanks fir the sensible thoughts, John. Given your caution about the style of the 15s, I’m curious how you found the 05, 09 and 10s on release?

posts.name: Alan Rath
posts.cooked:
John Morris:

How many of you who are loading up on '15 Northern Rhones have tasted them?

Until tonight, the only '15s I’d had were a couple of lovely St. Josephs (Barou and V. Paris). But my blind tasting group just tasted eight Northern Rhones and they were all VERY ripe. I mean REALLY ripe. Black cherry/blackberry jam.

John, I agree that 15 is a ripe vintage, I’ve tasted a decent number (just as 15 is a ripe vintage across Europe). I am also being a little circumspect in what I buy (partly because of vintage character, partly because of what I wrote earlier about escalating pricing, partly because I loaded up a bit more on the 13 vintage which I quite like). From what I’ve tasted so far, I actually think 12 is a riper, less interesting year, while 15 has the ripeness, but tends to be a little more controlled, more structured, and more interesting in general, exceptions will certainly apply.

I posted in another thread about the 15 Gonon Feray, which is a wonderful wine, particularly at its price point. The basic Faury St Joseph is very nice and VV quite good, Graillot’s Croze also quite good (and his St Joseph even better). Not a surprise that the Barruol wines are ripe, they tend that way even in cooler vintages. Haven’t tried any of the big boys out of bottle yet.

Your comments are part of what I wrote up-thread about there being a number of excellent California syrahs that can compete well with many N. Rhone wines, particularly in a vintage like 2015.

posts.name: John Morris
posts.cooked:
Pat Martin:

Thanks fir the sensible thoughts, John. Given your caution about the style of the 15s, I’m curious how you found the 05, 09 and 10s on release?

The 09s were ripe but not crazy ripe and I bought Gonon, Dard & Ribo, Bernard and Texier Brezeme and Seguret (a lot of the Seguret!) and some others.

I liked the '10s even more than the '09s on release and bought both Northern and Southern '10s (Levet, Balthazar, Alain Verset, Guigal B&B Cote Rotie, Jaboulet Dom. Thalabert, Graillot Crozes, Dom. Moulin-Tacussel CdP, Texier CdP, Texier Seguret, Coudelet de Beaucastel).

The wines tonight were nothing like those vintages at this stage. The best were really tasty, but in a fruit-forward, New World way.

But it’s my bed time. To be continued…

posts.name: Robert Alfert
posts.cooked:
Nicely done, John, and some valid notes of caution for those diving in.

It is a riper year. I prefer 2010 thus far. Based on the wines that I have had, limited as many of my producers are just coming out, and it’s sort of a cross between the structure of 2010 and the ripeness of 2009. The 2015 Gonon is a beauty, but yes, larger-scaled than normal. The Iles Feray is definitively very ripe but quite yummy and gulpable, not generally meant as an ager.

Barroul and Paris, IMHO, are not tradionalists to me. Barroul’s wine shows modern with some notable oak to me, Paris 30 and 60 are quite glossy, like Voge. I have not had the others in your line-up, but will comment that Souhaut, Balthazar and Faury are quite traditional to me.

I have bought the same producers that I have for some years, and agree with Fu, producer-specific purchases are in order:

Gonon
Benetiere
Juge
Levet
Clape Vin de Amis
Paris Geynale (show more traditional than 30/60)

Waiting for Jamet, Clape and Allemand, but may get sticker-shock.

And thanks for the hype-check. I will admit to falling prey, I’ve bought more of some bottlings than normal, and looking at others than I have not had in the past.

Sparks a similar debate as the 2009 or 2015 Beaujolais?

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked:
2015 Chave is coming out in the UK next month I think. Tempted to pick some up. Bought a case of Gonon last week too.

The '15 northern Rhône wines I’ve tried so far do seem to be in a bigger, richer, riper register; but I haven’t encountered anything as extreme as the most roasted 2015 Beaujolais that I’ve tasted.

posts.name: James Billy
posts.cooked:
William Kelley:

roasted 2015 Beaujolais.

And they definitely exist!

posts.name: David lo$n
posts.cooked:
Ive been buying my share (usually 4 bottles) and drinking 1. In general I’ve been trying to make sure I read that the wine has more red fruit or at least some red fruit but what’s funny is I’m really loving the ones that have that purply/violet flavor. But whether red or violet purple flavor I think it’s key in 2015 to make sure you research the wine has acidity because I think johns fear could be real.

Some of my takes on the wines I’ve drank are as follows (I think they will all improve to a good extent btw):

Balthazar Chaillot cornas - my number one disappointment. Big giant block of mass and I had a really hard time finding the acidity but it’s such a block I’m hoping everyone’s positivity on this board of what they tasted over the past few months trumps my views. Not bad just boring so far. Ok +

Paris geynale cornas - better than Chaillot Balthazar but a little boring. Maybe it’s just that block flavor of not giving a ton but the difference to me was it was fresher with more acidity slightly so I have more confidence in this then the Balthazar. But these first two wines I could easily see a normal person drinking and think we’re crazy for geeking on wines like we do but like a geek I could see them transform. Good

Gonon saint Joseph - Pretty big, more gamey/olivey than maybe all I drank so early but big time acidity and more dramatic fruit then the 2012 I had. Really felt it would age in its bigger package but will probably get really really gamey. Good / very good

Clusel Grandes places Cote rotie - had it same night as Gonon. In my opinion much better. Beautiful combo of fruit, acidity, texture. Earlier years of this wine sometimes are a bit light and closed and making you know it needs time or will never unfurl. This one is not like it at all. Very good +

Levet amerthyste Cote rotie - had in Cote rotie it’s their basic bottling. Visited ms levet and bought some bottles and she only offered this one. Lots of fruit with nice acidity but maybe too simple but it stayed purple red flavored to me which isn’t over the top. Really to me Totally a step down the other levets from other years but with that said the bigger fruit with the acidity makes me think the 2015 levet bigger ones could be nice. Good

Faurie bessard hermitage - my friend Vincent and I were quite amazed by this wine. First half hour it tasted like a 2015 Beaujolais but by an hour and a half in was all northern Rhône. Real beauty of not over the top but not too light. Very good +

Faury st Joseph vv saint Joseph - started off very light but after 3 or 4 hours blackened up like a cornas is said to. Definitely not over the top of ripeness and it transformed so much it must be an ager. Very very nice for the price but night and day versus the Gonon as if they are different regions. Good +

So my personal conclusions are that with proper research or knowledge I think a lot of these will age as beautiful wines on the larger scale of the Rhône equation. none of them had the big brambly blueberry quality of poor USA Syrah but I do think some of them can be boring if not done well picked too late bad terroir etc.

I really like the vintage and am very glad I’m going deep. The only thing I feel I will be missing is the red burgundy colored, clearer type of Cote rotie that other vintages of some wines can give that I haven’t seen so far possible with 2015.

posts.name: G. Shields
posts.cooked:
Great thread, really liking the discussion and insights being provided. Will try and add some brief notes and impressions from what i’ve tasted a little later when I get time.

In meanwhile, just a question to ask if folks have any experience of Johann Michel? Impressions? Style?

davidlown:

I’m not a buyer of 15s, but that note on Faurie gives me a little bit of encouragement to seek out some. Not much is posted about the producer, but I’m a bit of a fan as his late 1990s to 2001 vintages that I had, as they spoke pure Northern Rhone syrah to me.

posts.name: N@th@n V@nd€rgrift
posts.cooked:
When you guys talk about loading up on a vintage, what do you mean by that? Buying from lots of producers you don’t normally buy? I am expanding to a couple of new producers over the last few years, but that’s not a 2015 thing that’s more about being priced out/don’t get (I prefer to buy locally through official US importers for provenance reasons) My plan for 2015 is to buy from those folks I’ve been buying from (so far in 2015, the only thing I’ve bought more of than normal was Texier Côte-Rôtie). My cellar is currently 10.6% Northern Rhône which seems a bit high for my consumption.

How much high end syrah do you drink?

posts.name: Martin Steinley
posts.cooked:
Of course, Syrah accommodates heat and ripeness much better than Gamay and Pinot Noir. I have not found any 2015 northern Rhone to be too ripe, whereas I find almost all 2015 Beaujolais to be that way and some 2015 red Burgundies as well. That said, I see no reason whatsoever to back up the truck on 2015 northern Rhones. They are not better than any other recent vintage, just bigger and riper.

Disclaimer: I sell Allemand, Clape, Faurie, Faury, Gilles, Gonon, Levet, Lionnet and others.

posts.name: Markus S
posts.cooked:
Nathan V.:

When you guys talk about loading up on a vintage, what do you mean by that? … How much high end syrah do you drink?

I wouldn’t ask Alfert or Fu, as they polish off Levet, Allemand, and Juge every week, or so it seems.

posts.name: G. Shields
posts.cooked:
I like Martin’s take in the last post: maybe not always objectively better certainly a different expression. And to touch on an earlier question by a poster on where are the lighter coloured Cote Rôties, I guess the answer is with a 2014 or 2016 printed on the bottle!

I’ve not tasted any big names, but only a few wines came across as too much: Pierre Finon St-Jo Les Jouvencelles was really too ripe to my palate, coming across sweet and confected and the Laurent Combier Crozes was ripe and solid but just a bit one-dimensional.

That being said, i’ve tasted many a nice St Joseph from modest producers and they have been beautifully ripe, full bodied but with energy and balance. Great comparisons to 2015 Beaujolais where on the whole the ripeness just does not do it for me, in these cases for the N Rhônes it works to my palate. Some 2015 St Joseph’s that i’ve enjoyed are George Vernay Terres D’Encre, Éric & Joël Durand Les Coteaux (this was fab), and the Domaine du Tunnel St Jo.

For Cote Rôtie, tasted the Xavier Gerard CR and I loved it. Massive structure, powerful dark fruit and layers of bacon fat, I felt this has loads of potential and will need time to open up. The comparison to the 2014 was really interesting where the 14 is totally open, floral and elegant and in a very different register. Great to have both in the cellar I reckon!
